coredata.dc:description: Since the excess exposure to F- may induce serious issues to human health, the effective adsorption and sensitive detection of F- is essential. Therefore, carbon dots (CDs) capped CeO2 (CeO2@CDs) was synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of tannic acid and CeCl3. Due to abundant phenolic hydroxyl are reserved and excellent hydrophilicity, CeO2@CDs possess high F- adsorption capacity. The partition coefficient parameters (PC) are determined to be 2.65 L/g, which is comparable with previous work. The kinetics results and adsorption isotherm are consistent with pseudo-second-order model and Freundlich model, respectively, indicating the chemisorption dominate the adsorption, mainly via the ion exchange between hydroxyl and F-. Since phenolic hydroxyl existed on the CeO2@CDs, synergetic effect of CDs and CeO2 contribute to superior ROS eliminating capacity, even at acidic conditions. Moreover, due to the ROS scavenging of CeO2 @CDs abilities can be potentiated by F-, colorimetric detection of F- can be realized via horseradish peroxidase as an indicator. The linear range is 0.3–2.1 mM with limit of detection is 0.13 mg/L. The current results imply that CeO2@CDs possess potential in both efficient removal and sensitive detection of F- related contamination issues and elucidation of development to address other anions related issues.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots capped cerium oxide nanoparticles for highly efficient removal and sensitive detection of fluoride

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-constructed by the Province and Ministry, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science & Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since the excess exposure to F- may induce serious issues to human health, the effective adsorption and sensitive detection of F- is essential. Therefore, carbon dots (CDs) capped CeO2 (CeO2@CDs) was synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of tannic acid and CeCl3. Due to abundant phenolic hydroxyl are reserved and excellent hydrophilicity, CeO2@CDs possess high F- adsorption capacity. The partition coefficient parameters (PC) are determined to be 2.65 L/g, which is comparable with previous work. The kinetics results and adsorption isotherm are consistent with pseudo-second-order model and Freundlich model, respectively, indicating the chemisorption dominate the adsorption, mainly via the ion exchange between hydroxyl and F-. Since phenolic hydroxyl existed on the CeO2@CDs, synergetic effect of CDs and CeO2 contribute to superior ROS eliminating capacity, even at acidic conditions. Moreover, due to the ROS scavenging of CeO2 @CDs abilities can be potentiated by F-, colorimetric detection of F- can be realized via horseradish peroxidase as an indicator. The linear range is 0.3–2.1 mM with limit of detection is 0.13 mg/L. The current results imply that CeO2@CDs possess potential in both efficient removal and sensitive detection of F- related contamination issues and elucidation of development to address other anions related issues.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluoride (F-) contaminations in ground and drinking water have been considered as one of the most serious issues all over the world (He et al., 2020). Since it is essential for human beings to prevent dental caries and maintain healthy bones, introduction of F- in drinking water within the controlled degree is beneficial for human health. However, elevated concentration of F- may result in serious health problems, such as skeletal/dental fluorosis (Rose and Waite, 2002). According to the standard of World of Health Organization (WHO), the content of F- in drinking water should be lower than 1.5 mg/L (Chen, X. et al., 2021a), while this standard of China is 1.0 mg/L (Chen et al., 2017). Recently, more and more people are paying attention to the safety and quality of drinking water. Furthermore, the traditional methods mainly focus on removing or detecting F- from drinking water separately (Ahmadijokani et al., 2021).

Fluoride removal from water is an expensive and challenging process, due to its superior water solubility (Jagtap et al., 2012). Nowadays, common strategies for removal of F- include chemical precipitation (Huang, H. et al., 2017a), ion exchange (Zhang, C. et al., 2017a), membrane filtration (Zhang, J. et al., 2017b) and adsorption (Dhillon et al., 2017). Among these de-fluoridation strategies, adsorption by novel materials is superior in the aspects of simplicity, economic viability, sustainability and excellent efficiency (Wu, Y. et al., 2019b). The efficiency and industrial applicability of adsorbing materials largely depends on large surface area, high selectivity, higher chemical stability and lower cost (Ma et al., 2017), so adsorbent that fulfill these demands is urgently required.

To date, various materials have been applied for removal of fluoride from water, such as carbon based material, metal oxide, non-metal oxide, polymer and metal-organic frameworks (Ahmadijokani et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2022; Rashid et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). However, lower adsorption efficiency, separation issue, higher cost and crammed reaction conditions further limit their practical application (Chen, X. et al., 2021a).

According to hard and soft acid base theory, F-, as a hard Lewis base possess high electronegativity and small ionic size, would have a high affinity towards hard Lewis acid. Herein, rare earth metal, as a typical kind of hard Lewis alkaline, possess high efficiency in terms of F- removal (Lin et al., 2017). Among various rare earth elements, Cerium is the most abundant and cheapest one, and CeO2 is well known for its superior adsorption (Zhang et al., 2011), catalytic features (Zhang et al., 2017c) and polishing (He, 2018). Several work have reported that the removal efficiency of CeO2 is much better than the other adsorbents (Kang et al., 2017; Mobarak et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018a), so adjusting their morphology and features can achieve great potential for removing F-.

Carbon dots, as a kind of zero dimensional nanomaterials with size less than 10 nm, have emerged as a hot spot in scientific fields, due to their lower toxicity and environmental hazardous effect, especially for their fluorescent features (Chen et al., 2021b). However, since the surface of CDs is functionalized with abundant functional groups and polymer chains, they can exert excellent surface functions (Xu et al., 2020). When CDs are covered on poorly water-soluble nanoparticles, superior water solubility nanomaterials can be achieved via the electrostatic repulsion (He et al., 2015). Hence, CDs with abundant functional groups can be considered as effective nanomaterials to regulate the morphology and features of the CeO2.

Since F- is colorless, so detection of F- by naked eyes is quite difficult. To date, the common strategies for F- detection include ion chromatography, mass spectroscopy and ion-selective electrode, 19F nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (Ahmadijokani et al., 2021; Mohapatra and Das, 2019; Mohapatra et al., 2015). Most of these techniques usually require complicated operation procedures, rely on expensive apparatus and specialized technicians. Compared with above techniques, colorimetric methods for F- detection possess several benefits, such as doing not require sophisticated apparatus, specialized personnel, simple procedures and fast response (Yang et al., 2020). Nanozymes, indicated as engineered nanomaterials with inherent enzyme-like catalytic activities, which shows great potential in colorimetric assays for detecting various substances (Huang et al., 2019; Li and Liu, 2021; Wu et al., 2019a). In general, CeO2 nanoparticles are widely considered as typical mimic superoxide dismutase candidates (SOD), which can eliminate reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Xu and Qu, 2014). The ROS scavenging activity of CeO2 is associated with the electron shuttle between mixed valence states (Ce3+ and Ce4+). Since higher ratio of Ce3+/Ce4+ would result in higher content of oxygen vacancies that may contribute to higher ROS scavenging activities (Heckert et al., 2008). Liu’s group has reported that adsorption of F- on CeO2 would increase the content of Ce3+ and relevant oxygen vacancies (Zhao et al., 2019). And our previous work have found that CDs would eliminate the ROS and protect cells from oxidative stress (Xu et al., 2015a).

Based on above considerations, we attempted to fabricate the CDs-CeO2 hybrid nanomaterial to efficient removal and sensitive detection of F- from wastewater. Since previous work ascribed the adsorption of F- on CeO2 to the ion exchange between F- and metal surface hydroxide (M-OH) (Tao et al., 2020), tannic acid (TA), a ubiquitous natural polyphenol, with abundant hydroxyl was applied as raw materials to synthesize CDs. Furthermore, due to the excellent chelation ability of phenolic materials, TA can strongly coordinate to cerium (Guo et al., 2014) to regulate the size and feature of CeO2. And the surface of as prepared CDs capped CeO2 (CeO2@CDs) possess abundant hydroxyl which contribute to superior F- removal efficiency. Since the CeO2@CDs can inhibit the activity of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) via superior ROS scavenging activity which can be sensitized by F-, a colorimetric detection strategy towards F- was fabricated based on the inhibit degrees towards HRP activity.

Cerium chloride (CeCl3), HRP was purchased from Macklin Reagent Co. (China). TA, 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine dihydrochloride (≥98%) (TMB) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. (China). Hydrazine hydrate (85%), ethanol (99%), sodium hydroxide, acetic acid, hydrochloric acid (37%), ammonium hydroxide, NaF, NaAc, NaCl, NaBr, Na2CO3, Na2SO4, Na3PO4, NaHCO3 Na2HPO4 NaH2PO4 were purchased from Sinopharm Chem Reagent Co. (China). Tap water and lake water from sandy lake in Wuhan were selected as the real water sample for analyzing the adsorption efficiency of CeO2@CDs for F- removal. All reagents were of analytical grade and were used as received.

High resolution TEM images were observed by a JEOL JEM-2100 electron microscope operating at 200 kV. The surface areas were calculated by the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method, and the pore size distributions were obtained by the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) approach (JW-BK112, Beijing JWGB Sci. & Tech. Co., Ltd., China). The FL spectra were recorded by Cary Eclipse FL spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies Inc. USA) and UV–vis spectra were obtained using a Lambda Bio40 UV/vis spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). X ray diffraction (XRD) was measured by X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of all the samples were recorded on a Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on an ESCALAB 250Xi instrument (Thermo Scientific, USA). The Zeta potential were measured by Malvern Zeta sizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd, UK). The concentration of F- was measured by F- selective electrode (Shanghai Lei Ci Instrumentation, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of cerium dioxide coated with carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CeO2 @CDs is prepared by following process: 204 mg TA and 11.83 mg cerium (III) chloride were sequentially dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water under ultrasonic shaking. Then, 0.5 mL NH3·H2O, 0.625 mL of the hydrazine hydrate and 3.875 mL of the ethanol was added subsequently. After mixing together, the mixed solution was added into polytetrafluoroethylene liner and reacted at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained black solution was filtrated through 0.22 µm filter membrane. Then the obtained black solution was dialyzed against for deionized (MW=1000). Finally, the purified product was freeze-dried for the following application.

The initial concentration of F- were selected as 10−3.5 mol/L. The kinetic curve of fluoride adsorption was obtained by the following method: the prepared CeO2@CDs (300 mg) was added into a breaker containing 100 mL deionized water with 10−3.5 mol/L F-. The mixture was kept stirring at 25 °C, and 10 mL mixture was extracted and then filtrated through a 0.22 µm filter membrane for F- detection by the fluoride-selective electrode at different time (1 min, 5 min, 10 min, 30 min, 60 min, 180 min and 300 min). The removal efficiency of F- from the solution was determined by the following formula:

The adsorption of F- by CeO2@CDs was fitted by pseudo-first (Tseng et al., 2010), pseudo second-kinetic models (Huang et al., 2017b) and intra-particle diffusion model (Annadurai et al., 2002). The pseudo-first, pseudo second-kinetic models and intra-particle diffusion model formula were as follows:

Where Qt and Qe represent the amount of adsorption at time t and equilibrium, respectively. The k 1 (min−1) and k 2 (g/(mg·min)) are the pseudo-first and pseudo-second kinetic constants, respectively. And Kid (mg/(g.min1/2)) is the diffusion constant. C represents the thickness of liquid film.

The solution with different concentrations of F- were prepared with a volume of 15 mL and their pH values were adjusted to 3 by HCl and NaOH. Then 45 mg of the prepared CeO2 @CDs was added into each solution. The mixed solution was stirred for 12 h to achieve adsorption equilibrium and ultrafiltration were applied to remove adsorbent, then filtrated through 0.22 µm PES membrane to make adsorbent clean enough. Finally, the F- concentration in solution was measured to analyze the remaining F- concentration after adsorption. The equilibrium amount of F- adsorbed by the prepared CeO2@CDs was calculated by the following formula:

Where C0 and Ce represent the initial and equilibrium concentrations of F-, respectively (mg/L), while V is the volume of the solution (L) and m is the mass of the added CeO2@CDs (g).

Firstly, 15 mL of the aqueous solutions of F- (10−3.5 mol/L) were adjusted to different pH (3, 5, 7, 9 and 11) with HCl and NaOH. Then 45 mg of the prepared CeO2@CDs powder was added into each sample under stirring at 25 °C. After mixing for 12 h, the obtained samples were stand for 10 min and ultrafiltration were applied to remove adsorbent, then filtrated through 0.22 µm membrane to make adsorbent clean enough, then the remaining F- concentration was analyzed accordingly.

Firstly, 15 mL of F- containing aqueous solution (13 mg/L) were prepared. Then we added CO3 2-, Cl-, H2PO4 -, SO4 2-, NO3 -, Br- and PO4 3- with final concentration of 25 mg/L. After addition of 45 mg of the prepared CeO2@CDs powder under stirring for 12 h. Then mixture was stand for 10 min and ultrafiltration was applied to remove adsorbent, then filtrated through 0.22 µm filter membrane to make adsorbent clean enough. Finally, all the samples were checked to determine the left F- concentration after adsorption. We further investigated the effects of concentrations of anions (Cl-, SO4 2-, NO3 -, and PO4 3- as models) on the adsorption of F-. Their concentration was set as 25, 50, 75 and 100 mg/L.

The prepared CeO2@CDs with a final concentration of 25 μg/mL was added in the mixture of HPR enzymes (5 ng/mL), TMB (0.5 mM) and H2O2 (10 mM). The colorimetric reaction was proceeded in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0) at 25 °C for 20 min. The absorption spectrum of the samples was checked by an UV–visible spectrophotometer.

The prepared CeO2@CDs (25 μg/mL) was added into a range of solutions containing varied concentration F- (0.3–3.0 mM), after the CeO2@CDs and F- were mixed for 15 min. Then the HRP enzymes was mixed with above solution for 5 min, and finally the TMB and H2O2 were added into the mixture. After 1 min at 25 °C, the UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded accordingly. The process for studying the selectivity of detection was similar to above procedures in the presence of 1 mM interfering ions. And all the colorimetric detections were proceeded in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0) at 25 °C.

We prepared CeO2@CDs by hydrothermal method, which is facile, highly efficient and rapid technique to synthesize nanomaterials, including CeO2 and CDs. The TA may undergo hydrothermal cross-linking polymerization and further carbonization to form CDs with abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups. Since cerium ions (Ce3+) can form strong coordination bond with tannic acid (TA) in alkaline environments, then cerium sources (CeCl3) will nucleate and grow to form nano-scaled CeO2 with superior dispersibility via the formed CDs as the capping ligand, resulting in the formation of CDs capped CeO2. As shown in Fig. 1A and B, CeO2@CDs are quasi-spherical and monodisperse with narrow size distributions. The size of CeO2@CDs was mainly located between 40 and 50 nm via counting 100 particles for statistical analysis (Inset of Fig. 1A). The hydrodynamic size of pure CeO2@CDs is determined to be 160 nm, which is a little larger than that determined by TEM (Fig. S1). It is well known that light scattering strongly favors larger particles, and our results suggest that the CeO2@CDs sample is slightly aggregated. As shown in the Fig. 1 B, large amounts of small dots are observed on the spherical particles which implied the presence of carbon dots on the surface of CeO2. And clear HRTEM lattice fringe images of CeO2@CDs can also be observed (Fig. S2). The lattice spacing is determined to be 0.31 nm which can be assigned to the planar distance for CeO2 (111). The XRD patterns of the CeO2@CDs show several diffraction peaks at 28.6°, 47.4° and 56.2°, corresponding to (111), (220) and (311) lattice planes of the FCC crystalline lattice of CeO2 with the Fm-3 m space group (JCPDS card No. 34–0394) (Fig. 1C) (Poonia et al., 2019). Furthermore, the appearance of a broad peak centered at 21° implied the formation of amorphous carbon structure on the surface of CeO2. The presence of CDs can be further confirmed by the fluorescent assay. As shown in Fig. 1D, the CeO2@CDs exhibits an obvious peak centered at 495 nm under excitation of 380 nm which is quite close to that of the optimal excitation wavelength of the excitation spectra (dotted line). And the CeO2 @CDs shows an excitation dependent FL behavior, when the excitation wavelength increase from 340 to 420 nm at an interval of 20 nm, the emission peaks shift from the 446–508 nm, due to the existence of various surface defects (Xu et al., 2015b). According to the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm curves (Fig. 1E), obvious porous structure is observed and the specific surface area is determined to be 61.89 m2/g. And the pore size distribution of CeO2@CDs is presented in inset of Fig. 1E. It is very obvious that sizes of most pores are located within 20 nm while larger pores were also observed in the size distribution curves of the pore size distribution.

Then the surface chemistry of the CeO2@CDs was further characterized by the XPS. Several peaks located at 284.8, 399.8, 532.8 and 885.8 eV, which implied the presence of C, N, O and Ce elements on the surface of CeO2@CDs (Fig. S3). The related high resolution Ce3d was analyzed accordingly (Fig. 1F) and a complex structure was observed, due to the co-existence of both Ce3+ and Ce4+. Then the peaks were deconvoluted to two multiples with eight peaks after Gaussian fitting (She et al., 2018). The relative ratio of Ce4+ to Ce3+ is determined to be 2.40:1, and the higher ratio of Ce4+ may contribute to higher removing efficacy towards F- (Tao et al., 2020). Since CeO2@CDs was synthesized via a hydrothermal treatment in a high temperature and pressure environment, the reaction in high temperature and low oxygen partial pressures would result in removal of oxygen atom from the lattice surface of CeO2, which contribute to the formation of intrinsic oxygen vacancies (defects) (Schmitt et al., 2020). In general, an oxygen vacancy is formed, Ce4+ may be reduced to Ce3+ oxidation state by these generated two electrons (Sayle et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of adsorbent dosage and pH value
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of adsorbent dosages on removal efficiency of CeO2@CDs towards F- is shown in Fig. 2A, which suggested that with the adsorbent dosage increasing, the efficiency of adsorbent would also increase accordingly. And the related removal efficiency finally reaches plateau (around 90%) when the amounts of CeO2 @CDs further increase from 3 to 5 mg/mL. However, as for the trend of the Qt relative to dosage of CeO2@CDs, it is completely opposite to the removal efficiency mentioned above (red line). Since the concentration of F- is constant during the adsorption assay, while the vacant adsorption sites relative to un-adsorbed F- in aqueous solution are excessive at higher CeO2@CDs dosage (Wang et al., 2019b). Herein, 3 mg/mL adsorbent dosage was chosen for following experiments. As for a typical adsorption experiment, when the initial F- concentration is set as 10−3.5 mol/L and the dosage of the CeO2 @CDs is set as 3 mg/mL, about 89.59% fluoride can be removed and remaining concentration is determined to be 0.62 mg/L, which is much lower than the standard level for F- permitted in drinking water by WHO (1.5 mg/L) (Chen et al., 2021a). Moreover, the Qe of fluoride adsorbed by pure CDs is determined to be only 2.18 mg/g (Fig. S4), which is much smaller than that of CeO2 @CDs. This result confirmed the key role of CeO2 in the adsorption of fluoride.

Then the influence of initial pH on the removal of F- by CeO2@CDs was verified. Since the surface charge exhibits an essential role on the adsorption of F-, the Zeta potential of CeO2@CDs under different pH (from 2 to 9) in the presence and absence of F- (1 mM) was investigated (Fig. 2B). The charges of surface are positive under pH= 2 and 3 while it turns to negative when the pH further increase from 3 to 9. With the addition of F-, the Zeta potential of CeO2 @CDs turns to be more negative under different pH. The potential zero point (PZC) of CeO2@CDs is determined to be 3.63 and 3.32 in the absence and presence of F- (Song et al., 2019), which may indicate the existence of electrostatic forces between CeO2@CDs and F-. Then the adsorption efficiency under different pH was verified (Fig. 2 C). It is obvious that optimal adsorption was achieved at pH= 3. Although the surface of CeO2@CDs was most positively charged at pH= 2, its removal efficacy is much weaker than that of pH= 3, which may contribute to the formation of hydrofluoric acid. Then the availability of F- decreased obviously at pH= 2 (Wang et al., 2013), which contribute to the much lower removal capacity. Moreover, the hydrodynamic size increases a little as the pH is adjusted from 7 to 3 (Fig. S5A and 5B), which indicates that the CeO2@CDs is relatively stable under mild acidic conditions. The adsorption efficiency of CeO2@CDs towards F- under pH 3–9 is consistent with the trends of Zeta potential, which suggest the F- was removed by CeO2@CDs via electrostatic forces (Dhillon et al., 2017). However, both the adsorption efficiency and capacity are acceptable at different pH values, which suggest that the F- can be removed at these pH values to make it to be a promising F- adsorbent for future practical application. Herein, pH= 3 with the maximum removal efficacy was selected in the following experiments.

The effects of adsorption time on F- removal of CeO2@CDs are carried out to reflect the adsorption kinetics ( Fig. 3A). It is obvious that the adsorption rate of F- increase rapidly at initial 15 min and gradually slows down within 15 and 60 min. And the highest adsorption capacity of CeO2 @CDs for F- (11.60 mg/g) was saturated at about 60 min. When the incubation time further increases to 5 h, the adsorption capacity remains constant, thus 60 min is considered as adsorption equilibrium time and it is utilized in the following assay. Then the removal behavior is further analyzed by pseudo-first order, pseudo-second order kinetics models to fit the experimental data. The fitting results were presented in Fig. 3B and S6. Due to the higher correlation coefficient (R2 =0.9999), the kinetics of CeO2@CDs for adsorbing F- would be well fitted by the pseudo-second-order model, indicating that the surface adsorption reaction dominate and control the whole stage while the chemisorption was the rate controlling step. It is obvious that the adsorption capacity (Qe) obtained from the fitting of pseudo-second kinetics model (11.76 mg/g) is identical to that determined by the experimental one (Qe=11.72 mg/g) (Table S1).

The adsorption rate control mechanism can be fitted by an intraparticle diffusion model. There is a possibility of the diffusion of the adsorbable ions from the solution into the pores of the sorbent to be a rate controlling step. The plot of the amount of adsorbed fluoride versus t1/2 is presented in inset of Fig. 3A. The plot can be divided into three parts, namely initially curved part, followed by other linear part and a smoothly plateau, which is related to the boundary layer diffusion effects, intra-particle diffusion and adsorption equilibrium stage (Annadurai et al., 2002). In addition, since the linear plots do not pass through the origin line, indicating that the both the intra-particle diffusion and boundary layer diffusion effects dominate the removal rate of the adsorption process (Namasivayam and Ranganathan, 1993).

In order to further understand adsorption mechanism and relationship between adsorbent and adsorbate, the adsorption isotherm of CeO2@CDs was investigated. The obtained experimental data was analyzed by the Freundlich and Langmuir isotherm models, which are typical models to analyze the adsorption equilibrium. The Langmuir model is generally to describe the homogeneous and monolayer adsorption without interactions between adsorbed substances (He et al., 2019). And the Freundlich model is an empirical equation assume the heterogeneous adsorption system with multi adsorption (Tchomgui-Kamga et al., 2010). The experimental data fitted with the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms models and related parameters were presented in Fig. 3C and D, and Table 1. Since no plateau is observed in fitted data of Freundlich model and the regression coefficient value (R2) of fitted Freundlich model is higher than that of Langmuir model, the equilibrium isotherm is relative better described by the heterogeneous adsorption model. However, due to the limited adsorption capacity which result in the relatively large experimental deviations, each model exhibits a relative bad linear relationship (Chen et al., 2022). It should be noted that the removal process of F- is dominated by formation of stable chemical bonds, such as that between Ce-OH and F-, rather than simply physical adsorption. Furthermore, the n derived from the fitting curves are larger than 1, indicating the strong affinity existed between the CeO2@CDs and F- (Zhang et al., 2019). In addition, the values of KF increase obviously with the increasing of the experimental temperature, suggesting the adsorption process can be promoted by the temperature. This trend proves that the removal process is an endothermic process. The larger value of K F may reflect the more obvious combining capacity (Boldaji et al., 2009).

The thermodynamic parameters, such as Gibb’s free energy (ΔG), enthalpy (ΔH) and the entropy change (ΔS) are further determined to evaluate the thermodynamic feasibility of the adsorption process. These parameters are usually determined based on the following formulas:
(6)
Δ G = − R T

(7)

(8)
Δ G = Δ H − T Δ S

As shown in Table. S2, the negative Gibb’s free energy change confirms the spontaneity of F- adsorption by CeO2@CDs. In addition, with the increasing of temperature, ΔG decreases accordingly, implying the positive effect of temperature on the adsorption process. The positive value of ΔH (26.33 KJ/mol) also confirms the spontaneity of the process. Moreover, the positive value of ΔS indicated that the structure of CeO2@CDs changes obviously after the adsorption of F-, which result in the degree of disorderness increases obviously at the solid-liquid interface (Song et al., 2018).

Then adsorption performance of the CeO2@CDs was compared with several previous work (Table S3). Although the adsorption capacity of CeO2@CDs is much lower than previous work, while the adsorption capacity and removal efficiency is highly depend on the operating conditions. Hence, in order to better understand the real adsorption performance of each adsorbent, partition coefficient parameters (PC) of CeO2@CDs for F- was determined and compared with results of previous work, due to it does not rely on the initial F- concentration and adsorbent dosage. The PC (L/g) was determined by the following equation (Ahmadijokani et al., 2021):
(9)
P C =
Q
e
C
e
Where Qe (mg/g) and Ce (mg/L) represents the adsorption capacity values and concentration of F- at equilibrium, respectively. As listed in Table S3, the PC value of CeO2@CDs is much larger than most previous work, which indicating superior adsorption performance of our work.

As we mentioned in the pH adsorption studies (Fig. 2C), the optimal F- removal is achieved at pH= 3 (>PZC, 3.63) and the satisfied removal efficacy (about 36.9%) can be achieved at pH= 9.0, which implying that not only electrostatic attraction is involved in F- adsorption by CeO2@CDs while chemical adsorption is also responsible for removal of F-. Due to adsorption mechanism is mainly via the chemical adsorption, the hydrodynamic size remains constant after the adsorption of F- (Fig. S7). The EDX mapping of CeO2@CDs adsorbed by F- confirms that the F- is uniformly distributed on the surface of CeO2@CDs, which suggest that F- can be effectively enriched by the CeO2@CDs ( Fig. 4A).

In order to further verify the adsorption mechanism, the XPS spectra of CeO2@CDs in the absence or presence of F- are analyzed. Compared with the XPS spectra of CeO2@CDs in the absence of F- (Fig. S3), an additional peak centered at 685.1 eV appeared, due to F 1 s, which imply the presence of chemical bound F- in ceria lattice and the adsorption of F- on the surface of CeO2@CDs (Fig. 4B and Fig. S8).

Several works reported that the surface hydroxyl on the metal oxides is essential and most active adsorption sites for removal of anions from aqueous environments (Wang et al., 2013). As shown in the FTIR spectra of CeO2@CDs before adsorption of F-, a broad peak located around 3417 cm−1 is observed, due to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl (Fig. 4C). And the two peaks centered at 1112 and 1120 cm−1 are attributed to the bending vibration of hydroxyl on cerium ions (Zhang et al., 2005). And the intense peak at 1625 and 1398 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of CO and C-N, respectively. Herein, the surface of CeO2 is decorated with abundant hydroxyl functionalized CDs, due to CDs are synthesized by natural polyphenols. After the adsorption of F- by CeO2@CDs, the morphology of the peak from the stretching vibration of hydroxyl changes obviously, implying that the surface hydroxyl was involved in the adsorption of F- (Deng et al., 2011). In addition, the peaks at 1112 and 1120 cm−1 disappeared and an additional peak centered 1207 cm−1 appeared, which indicate the substitution of hydroxyl by F- to form the Ce-F bonds (Zirpe et al., 2020). The above mechanism of F- adsorption by CeO2@CDs was further investigated by recording the high resolution XPS spectra of O1s (Fig. 4D and E). Both the O1s signal of CeO2@CDs in the absence or presence of can be deconvoluted into two components. The area ratio of M-OH decreases from 62.9% to 53.2% after the adsorption by F-, while the proportion of M-O increases from 37.1% to 46.8%. Moreover, it should be noted that the pH of solution increase from 3.00 to 3.39 after the adsorption of F-, due to the replacement of OH- groups by F- on the surface of CeO2 @CDs (Chen et al., 2022). Hence, we speculate that the mechanism of F- adsorption by CeO2@CDs is via the chemical adsorption and ion exchange between hydroxyl of CDs decorated on CeO2 and F-.

In general, multiple anions, such as CO3 2-, Cl-, H2PO4 -, SO4 2-, NO3 -, Br- and PO4 3-, are existed in F- containing wastewater, which may interfere the adsorption of F- (Mo et al., 2018). Hence, investigation of their possible influence on the adsorption of F- is essential for the practical application of CeO2@CDs. The potential interferences by co-existing ions are verified by introduction of individual interfering anions (25 mg/mL) to the adsorption system (adsorbent dose=3 mg/mL and C0 =13 mg/L). As shown in Fig. S9, the removal efficacy towards F- in the presence of interference ions is comparable to that in the absence of anions, with slight decreasing. Then we further investigate the influence of various anions with different concentration on the removal of F-. Then we chose four anions, including Cl-, SO4 2-, NO3 -, and PO4 3-, as model to investigate the effects of concentrations on the adsorption of F-. As shown in Fig. 5A, with increasing of various anions, the F- adsorption amount decrease slightly. When the concentration of various anions reaches 100 mg/L, the adsorbent amount of F- decreases about 14.0% for Cl-, 6.3% for SO4 2-, 11.1% for NO3 - and 13.0% for PO4 3-. This may be caused by the competitive adsorption of co-existing anions towards active sites on the surface CeO2 @CDs with increasing of concentration, thus blocking the active interaction between F- and CeO2@CDs, thus contributing to the lower adsorption efficiency. Since the selected anions are mostly ‘hard’ based and though Ce is a ‘hard’ acid, the influence of ‘soft’ bases has also studied. As shown in Fig. S10., three kinds of soft bases (S2-, S2O3 2- and I-) show little influence on the adsorption of fluoride. Therefore, CeO2@CDs possess superior selectivity to remove F- in complex practical environment, regardless of other co-existing anions. Then we further examine the removal efficacy of CeO2@CDs towards F- in different practical aqueous environment, such as tap water and lake water from sandy lake in Wuhan. The results implied that the Qe of F- is 11.10 and 10.49 mg/g in tap water and lake water, respectively (Fig. 5B), which is very close to that of adsorption in pure water. And the related adsorption efficiency of F- was determined to be 93.11% and 87.99%, respectively. Herein, the CeO2@CDs exhibits excellent capacity to remove F- in the presence of other interfering anions and great feasibility of adsorbing F- under practical situations, which show excellent potential for future industrial waste water application.

It is quite apparent that the cyclic performance of adsorbent is an important index to evaluate its application performance. The desorption of F- by CeO2@CDs was performed by NaOH which is similar to previous work (Wang, F. et al., 2019a). We have found that about the desorption efficiency can reach 96% when the concentration of NaOH is set as 0.5 mol/L. Moreover, the CeO2@CDs showed superior cyclic performance, and the adsorption capacity always kept fairly high (8.23 mg/g) after fifth consecutive reuse (Fig. S11), which imply the superior regeneration performance and its feasibility can be further extended for industrial treatment of F- containing wastewater.

Since both the CeO2 and phenolic hydroxyl group functionalized CDs possess superior ROS scavenging activity and the ROS scavenging activity can be sensitized by the F-. And HRP is a typical natural peroxidase (POD) that can catalyze the H2O2 to oxidize the TMB to generate a distinct blue color with an absorption peak at 652 nm. Hence, colorimetric detection of F- can be realized by their ROS scavenging activity and the HRP with superior POD activity as an inner indicator (Scheme S1). Firstly, the concentration of HRP is optimized to make the absorbance less than 1 that can satisfy the Lambert-Beer’s law. It is very obvious that the absorbance gradually increases with the increase of the concentration of HRP. When the concentration reaches 10 ng/mL, the absorbance is larger than 1 (Fig. S12). Hence, 5 ng/mL HRP was used in the following experiments to achieve higher detection sensitivity. Then the feasibility of F- detection by the proposed system was examined. As shown in Fig. 6A, the POD activity of HRP can be obviously inhibited by CeO2@CDs and the incubation of CeO2@CDs with 1 mM F- would further potentiate the ROS scavenging activity of CeO2@CDs. However, the simple introduction of F- shows no influence on the catalytic efficiency of HRP, indicating the essential role of CeO2 @CDs in ROS scavenging. Moreover, we have found that both CeO2 @CDs and F- pretreated CeO2 @CDs do not induce formation of a blue colored product, implying that the POD activity is not observed in CeO2@CDs.

Since the surface of CeO2 is decorated with abundant phenolic hydroxyl functionalized CDs and the phenolic hydroxyl usually shows high ROS scavenging activity, so the role of surface CDs in detection of F- should be excluded. Although the POD activity of HRP can be suppressed by the pure CDs, its inhibition ability is much weaker than that of the CeO2@CDs, indicating the synergetic effect of CDs and CeO2 in eliminating the ROS (Fig. S13). However, the incubation of CDs with F- results in none enhancement towards their ROS scavenging ability, which further confirmed the important function of CeO2 in detection of F-.

Since the catalytic efficiency of HRP and CeO2 @CDs is highly correlated with the pH, then the influence of buffer pH on the POD and ROS scavenging activity is also optimized accordingly. As depicted in Fig. 6B and D, the POD activity of HRP increases as the pH increases from 3 to 5 and reaches maximum at pH= 5. When the pH further increases, the POD activity drops obviously and keeps constant within the pH range from 6 to 10. As expected, in the presence of CeO2 @CDs, similar trends have been observed while the POD activity can be totally inhibited within pH range from 6 to 10 (Fig. 6C and D). Since the colorimetric detection of F- is based on the F- sensitized ROS scavenging activity of CeO2 @CDs and maintain the linear relationship, so pH= 4 is selected for the detection of F-.

In general, the enzymatic activity of CeO2 nanoparticles is not unidirectional, and both reduction and oxidation of surface cerium ion are highly depending on the pH as well as redox potential of the species involved. As for the ROS scavenging capacity, CeO2 nanoparticles can reduce superoxide radicals at neutral pH to hydrogen peroxide, while the CeO2 nanoparticles possess oxidase-like activity that is associated with the potential to cause the oxidative damage at acidic pH (Asati et al., 2009; Korsvik et al., 2007; Lord et al., 2021). However, the POD activity of HRP can be suppressed by CeO2 @CDs at highly acidic conditions, which resulted from the surface phenolic hydroxyl functionalized CDs with superior antioxidant activity and great F- enrichment effects.

In order to further optimize the detection conditions, the concentrations of TMB, H2O2 and CeO2 @CDs are optimized. As shown in Fig. S14A and S14B. the typical absorption at 652 nm of each group increased obviously with the concentration of TMB and H2O2. In order to obtain good linear relationship, the optimum concentrations of TMB and H2O2 are set as 0.5 and 10 mM, respectively. However, the absorbance at 652 nm is negatively correlated with the concentration of CeO2 @CDs (Fig. S14C). When the concentration of CeO2 @CDs is set as 25 μg/mL, the absorption is very close to 1, so 25 μg/mL CeO2 @CDs is used for the detection of F- which can balance the signal intensity and the detection sensitivity.

Since the antioxidant activity of CeO2 @CDs can be obviously sensitized by F-, a colorimetric detection strategy was exploited for F- detection by HRP as indicator. The UV–vis absorption spectra of HRP-CeO2 @CDs system in the presence of different F- are presented in Fig. 7A, With the concentration of F- increase from 0 to 3 mM at an intervals of 0.3 mM, the absorbance at 652 nm decreases regularly and a great linearity relationship between ΔA/A0 and F- concentration is observed within the range between 0.3 and 2.1 mM. However, with the further addition of F- to 3 mM, the value of ΔA/A0 depart from initial linearity and reaches plateau (Fig. 7B), indicacting the vacant adsorption sites of CeO2 @CDs can be fully occupied by 2.1 mM F-. Herein, the concentration of F- can be quantitatively determined by above linear relationship with a linear equation: ΔA/A0 = 0.283 × [F-]+ 0.0935, R2 = 0.995 (Inset of Fig. 7B). The limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 3.08 μM (equal to 0.13 mg/L) which is comparable to most previous work (Table S4). And the LOD is much lower than acceptable level for F- permitted in drinking water by WHO (1.5 mg/L) (Chen et al., 2021a). Since the detection performance is highly related to the ROS scavenging activity of the CeO2 @CDs. The much better detection performance can be realized via elaborate modulated the ROS scavenging activity of the CeO2 @CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of F- detection
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is another important factor of analysis system that should be considered, due to interferences from other co-existing anions would induce false positive results in practical applications. As shown in Fig. S15A and S15B, the POD activity of HRP can be seriously retarded by CeO2@CDs in the presence of F- while the presence of other anions except for F- would not induce any inhibition to the POD activity of HRP. In order to further examine the selectivity for detection of F-, the UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB catalyzed by HRP and H2O2 in the presence of F- co-existed with other anions are studied (Fig. S15C and S15D). It is obvious that POD ability of HRP can be dramatically inhibited by F-, regardless the presence of other anions. Moreover, the absorbance is comparable to each other and not affected by the presence of other anions. Herein, this assay demonstrates the feasibility and high selectivity of the HRP-CeO2 @CDs system for detection of F- in practical complicated conditions.

It is generally accepted that oxygen vacancies play an vital role in the ROS scavenging activity of CeO2 nanoparticles. For the CeO2, the Ce3+ sites are accupied with oxygen vacancies, and each OV corresponds to two Ce3+ sites (Anderson and Steitz, 1975). The changes of the surface oxidation stae of Ce3+ (or Ce4+) after adsorption by F- are also highly visible in the deconvoluted Ce 3d XPS spectrum. After adsorption of F-, the concentration of Ce3+ increases from 29.4% to 40.4% ( Fig. 8 A), which indicates the generation of additional oxygen vacancies in the ceria lattice after the adsorption of F- (Ahmad et al., 2014). The increasing of the oxygen vacancies can be also confirmed by the Raman spectra. As shown Fig. 8B, the presence of obvious band at 459 cm−1 is attributed to the symmetrical stretching of fluorite type structure (Li et al., 2014). After adsorption of F-, another two peaks at 245 cm−1, 597 cm−1 appeared, which implied the concentration of oxygen vacancies in CeO2 nanoparticles improves obviously (Wang et al., 2018b). Moreover, the phenolic functionalized CDs might faciliate the adsorption of F- onto CeO2 @CDs via chemical adsorption and ion exchange, which further increased the concentration of Ce3+ and the accompanying oxygen vacancies (Zhao et al., 2019). Herein, these results indicated the detection of F- is mainly based on F- sensitized ROS scavenging activity of CeO2 @CDs after adsorption of F-, due to enhanced oxygen vacancy content after adsorption of fluoride.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of F- in the real environment
ce:section.ce:para: Based on above results, this colorimetric analytical platform of HRP-CeO2 @CDs towards F- detection exhibits several advantages (superior selecivity, simplicity and high sensitivity), which inspires us to apply this platform in practical water samples. The real tap water and sandy water were filtered through 0.22 µm PES membrane and then spiked with different concentration (0.5 and 1.5 mM) of F-. As depicted in Table 2, the determined concentration is very close to that of initial spiked concentration. The recoveries for determing F- in estimated to be about 99%. However, the detection performance in sandy lake water, as a much complex environment, is worse, while their recoveries increases to 96–118%, which may also satisfy the practical application. This result indicats that the developed colorimetric nanosensor exhbits potential application for practical water analysis. More importantly, the novel strategy is much simpler and environmental friendly, due to the absence of toxic QDs, organic dyes, and organic solvents.

In this work, the CDs capped CeO2 (CeO2@CDs) have been fabricated to simultaneous and effective removal and sensitive detection of F- which are essential for prevention and control of F- contamination. The adsorption equilibrium can be reached within 60 min and the kinetics of adsorption is via the pseudo second order model. The adsorption capacity is determined to be 11.60 mg/g and the PC is found to be 2.65 L/g, which is comparable to most other previous work. The F- was adsorbed by CeO2 @CDs via the Freundlich model and adsorption mechanism by CeO2 @CDs is the chemical adsorption and ion exchange between hydroxyl of CDs decorated on CeO2 and F-. The presence of other co-existing anions does not show any influence on the adsorption of F- and detection of F- in practical water samples have been realized by the CeO2 @CDs. Since the ROS scavenging activity of CeO2 @CDs can be sensitized by the F-, colorimetric detection of F- can be achieved by their ROS scavenging activity and the HRP with superior POD activity as an inner indicator. The related conditions for detection of F- have been optimized to achieve superior detection performance. The linear range is determined to be 0.3 mM and 2.1 mM and the LOD is estimated to be 3.08 μM (0.13 mg/L), while the detection performance is comparable to other works. This CeO2 @CDs nanomaterial possess great potential in the removal and detection of F- in future wastewater treatment.

The presence of excess fluoride in drinking water would result in dental and skeletal fluorosis among other health issues. Therefore, sensitive and selective detection of fluoride and accordingly removal from drinking water are of great significance. The CDs capped CeO2 nanoparticles with abundant hydroxyl was synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of TA, as natural polyphenols, and its superior coordination capacity towards cerium. Since their ROS scavenging can be potentized by the F-, colorimetric detection strategy towards F- was achieved with HRP as an indicator. And the surface abundant hydroxyl contributes to their superior F- removal efficiency.

Cao Li: Result interpretation. Bingbing Jiang: Writing – review & editing. Ziqiang Xu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
